Threat.value: Taking too many risks or doing things that could cause you harm
Irritable behavior, angry outbursts, or acting aggressively

Memory.value: My memories of the frightening event are like a film that plays over and over.
I can talk about what happened very easily.
When I have memories of what happened I sometimes hear things in my head that I heard during the frightening event.

Negative Appraisals.value: I will not be able to control my anger and will do something terrible
The world is a dangerous place
I feel like I don't know myself anymore

Maladaptive Strategies.value: ruminate about how event could have been prevented

vignette:
He is an 80-year-old South Asian man, married and previously described by family as steady and reserved, who now presents with a high level of post-traumatic stress symptoms, with a PCL-5 score of 64. His difficulties centre on a suicide-related trauma that continues to intrude on everyday life. He describes the event in a way that is strikingly vivid and repetitive, saying that the memories are like a film that plays over and over. He can speak about what happened with unusual ease, and at times he also hears fragments of the original sounds in his head, as if the scene has not fully ended.

Day to day, ordinary sensory reminders keep the experience close. Related smells, especially a burnt smell, are particularly unsettling for him and quickly heighten his sense of threat. At those times he becomes more irritable, more easily angry, and less able to concentrate. He also notices a tendency to take risks or do things that could place him in harm’s way, which appears alongside this heightened state of tension. In the same moments, the event returns to mind with particular force, and the sense of danger becomes difficult to shake.

He now holds grim views about himself and the world. He fears that he will not be able to control his anger and may do something terrible. He sees the world as a dangerous place and says he feels as if he does not know himself anymore. These appraisals sit heavily with him and seem to keep both the memories and the sense of current threat close at hand.

In coping, he ruminates repeatedly about how the event might have been prevented, avoids hospitals, ambulances and physical examinations, and uses drugs. These strategies appear to have narrowed his world rather than settled it. They have left him more preoccupied, less engaged, and increasingly strained in his marriage and wider functioning, with the trauma still organising much of his daily life.
